turns.text: Hello Eugene, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*settles back in chair, a small smile crossing my face*

Thank you for asking me. It's... nice to be asked, actually.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Yes, we're ready to start our family. My wife and I have talked about this for years, and it finally feels like the right time. I had my first checkup a few months ago, and the doctor said everything looks good—I'm low risk, healthy, all of that. But honestly? That label doesn't quiet my mind the way I thought it would.

*fidgets with hands*

I spend a lot of time reading about pregnancy, about how a baby develops week by week. It's miraculous, truly, but it also terrifies me. All these intricate processes happening that I have no control over. I read about neural tubes and organogenesis and I think, "How does a body just... know how to do all of this?"

*looks up*

The hardest part is that I don't have my mother here. Back home, she would know things, or at least I could ask her. Here, I feel quite alone with all these questions. My doctor is kind, but our visits feel very... clinical. She checks boxes on a form, tells me I'm fine, and I leave feeling like I haven't really been heard.

I want someone to understand that being "low risk" doesn't mean I'm not anxious. Does that make sense?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

*leans forward, chin resting on hand*

You know, I think I had this image in my head—naive, perhaps—of what it would be like. I imagined my doctor would sit with me, really explain things. Not just the clinical facts, but... the wonder of it all. I thought she would know that I'm new here, that I don't have family nearby, and that I might need more reassurance than someone else.

*voice becomes quieter*

I thought there would be time. Unhurried conversations where I could ask the questions that keep me awake at night. Questions about what could go wrong, yes, but also questions about what's happening at each stage. I wanted to feel like a partner in this, not just a body being monitored.

*shifts in seat*

The reality has been... different. The appointments are fifteen, maybe twenty minutes. The nurse asks her questions—do you smoke, do you exercise, family history—and I answer them all honestly. Then the doctor comes in, reviews my chart, tells me I'm "low risk" like it's a golden ticket, and we're done. In and out.

*looks down*

What I'm realizing is that being told I'm healthy isn't the same as being cared for. I need someone to see that I'm anxious about invisible things—the things I can't control. That I need education, yes, but also... presence. Someone who understands what it means to be far from home, starting this journey alone.

*pauses*

Does that sound ungrateful? I don't mean it to.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

*takes a deep breath, eyes widening slightly*

The biggest surprise? How... dismissive it felt when I brought up my anxiety.

*looks away for a moment*

I remember sitting in that exam room, and I tried to explain—I said something like, "I've been reading a lot about fetal development, and I'm worried about things going wrong during the early weeks." And my doctor, she looked up from her clipboard and said, "Well, you're young and healthy. Most pregnancies are fine."

*voice tightens*

"Most." That word just hung there. I wanted to say, "But what about the ones that aren't? What if mine is one of them?" Instead, I just nodded and said okay.

*rubs face*

The surprise was that she seemed almost... impatient? Like my worry was something to be checked off rather than understood. She didn't ask *why* I was anxious, didn't explore it. Just reassured me with statistics and moved on to the next patient.

*looks up*

What I wasn't prepared for was how lonely that made me feel. Back home, when you're concerned about something like this, people gather around you. They listen. Here, I felt like I was being told my feelings were inconvenient—that because my blood work is good and my age is "optimal," there's nothing to discuss.

*voice softens*

The positive surprise? That she scheduled the appointment at all. That someone was willing to see me before conception. At least there's that.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

*pauses, looking down at hands*

I thought I would feel... ready. Like flipping a switch. My wife and I have talked about this for years, we're stable, we have a home—even if it's far from everything I know. I expected to feel excited, maybe nervous in a normal way, but fundamentally... prepared.

*voice becomes uncertain*

What I didn't expect was this fog. This constant, low-level anxiety that I can't quite shake. I thought once we decided to try, I would feel grounded, but instead I feel like I'm suspended in something uncertain. Some days I'm reading about fetal development with genuine wonder, imagining our child, and other days I'm spiraling—thinking about all the ways things could go wrong, all the things my body might not know how to do.

*looks up, eyes slightly glassy*

The hardest part is the isolation. I kept that expectation that being "ready" meant being emotionally stable, and I am—I love my wife, I want this. But I'm also grieving, in a strange way. I'm grieving my mother's absence, my sisters not being here to celebrate with me, the community I left behind. And somehow that grief is tangled up with my excitement about the pregnancy.

*voice cracks slightly*

I expected to feel like I was building something strong. Instead, I feel... fragile. Like I'm supposed to be this confident woman starting a family, but I'm terrified I don't have the right support to do it well.

*pauses*

Does that make sense? That you can be ready and not ready at the same time?
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

*sits back, eyes closing for a moment*

Everything. I think everything is missing.

*opens eyes, speaking slowly*

From my doctor—I need her to ask about the emotional weight of this, not just the physical readiness. I need her to understand that "low risk" doesn't touch the real fears. I need time. Real conversations, not a checklist. And I need her to acknowledge that my anxiety isn't a problem to be solved; it's normal, and it's valid, and it deserves space in the room.

*voice becomes heavier*

From my partner—my wife is wonderful, truly. But she didn't grow up here either, and she's busy finding work. We're both adjusting. Sometimes I feel like I'm burdening her with all my questions about fetal development and my worries. She tries to reassure me, but I can feel her own stress underneath it. We're both new here, both trying to build something, and sometimes that feels isolating together.

*rubs temples*

From my wider network... there is no network. That's the truth of it. My mother isn't here. My sisters aren't here. The women who would normally gather around me and share their stories—I don't have them. In my community back home, you're never alone with this. People check on you, they bring food, they sit with you and say, "I was scared too."

*voice quiets*

Here in Needham, everyone is polite but distant. I don't know other women trying to conceive. I don't have anyone to call at midnight when the anxiety gets loud.

*pauses*

I need community. I need that more than anything else.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

*leans forward intently, as if this question has been waiting*

Oh, I looked. I looked so much.

*laughs softly, a bit self-conscious*

I expected to find answers. Clear, reassuring information about what to expect, how to prepare my body, what milestones to watch for. I thought there would be something like... a roadmap. Week by week, here's what's happening, here's what you should do.

*voice becomes animated*

And there is that, yes. I found apps, websites, forums full of women sharing their experiences. The diagrams of fetal development—those are beautiful, exactly what I wanted. I can trace the formation of the heart, understand the neural tube. It feeds something in me, this need to comprehend the miracle of it all.

*expression shifts, becoming troubled*

But what I also found was... chaos. Contradictions. One source says you need prenatal vitamins three months before conception, another says it doesn't matter. One forum is full of women terrified about miscarriage at six weeks, another is celebrating successful pregnancies. And all of it is mixing together in my head—the scientific facts and the fear and the hope.

*voice tightens*

What I actually needed was someone to help me sort through it. To say, "This information is reliable, trust this. This is anxiety talking, let's address that." Instead, I'm drowning in information but still feeling entirely unprepared.

*looks directly at you*

The apps are impersonal. They don't know I'm alone here. They don't know about my specific fears or my grief. They just... count down.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

*sits up straighter, eyes narrowing with frustration*

The biggest gap? It's the gap between information and meaning.

*gestures expressively*

Online, I read these detailed descriptions of organogenesis—how the primitive streak forms, how the mesoderm differentiates. There are animations, timelines, beautiful illustrations. It's presented like this extraordinary scientific process, which it is. The websites explain the "why" behind things, the purpose of each stage.

*voice becomes harder*

My doctor just says, "Take your prenatal vitamins. Don't smoke. Maintain a healthy weight." She treats it like... maintenance. Like I'm preparing a car for inspection, not nurturing the beginning of a human life.

*pauses, struggling with the words*

What the digital sources don't tell me—and what my doctor also doesn't tell me—is how to hold both things at once. The miracle and the risk. The scientific understanding and the emotional weight. Online, I can read about neural tube defects, which is important, but then I spiral into catastrophizing. My doctor dismisses my worry as unnecessary because my risk is low.

*voice softens, becoming almost vulnerable*

Neither of them is asking: "Eugene, how are you actually feeling about this? What do you need to feel safe and supported?" 

The gap isn't really between the sources. It's between all the information in the world and the actual human being sitting in front of them—scared, isolated, trying to make sense of it all.

*looks down*

I think I need someone to bridge that gap.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

*hesitates, then shakes head*

I don't have a pre-existing health condition. My doctor was very clear about that—I'm healthy, no complications, nothing to manage. I should feel lucky, I suppose.

*pauses, a slight frown crossing face*

But sometimes I wonder if that's made things harder in a strange way. Because I don't have a "problem" to point to, I feel like my anxiety doesn't warrant the same attention. If I had diabetes or hypertension, there would be a clear protocol, right? Regular monitoring, medication adjustments, a care plan. My doctor would have to take it seriously.

*fidgets with hands*

Instead, because I'm "low risk," I feel like I'm supposed to just... move forward smoothly. No complications means no special support needed. But the invisible things—the emotional weight, the isolation, the cultural displacement—those don't show up on bloodwork.

*voice becomes quieter*

Sometimes I think about my sisters back home. If one of them had anxiety like mine, the family would rally. There would be remedies, rituals, people checking in. Here, there's... nothing. No protocol for someone who is physically healthy but emotionally struggling.

*looks up*

I think what I'm realizing is that maybe I needed to have a condition for the system to see me as needing care. Does that sound terrible? I'm not wishing I were sick. I just wish being anxious and alone was enough of a reason for someone to slow down and really help me.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

*lets out a long, tired laugh*

I had to ask for everything. Every single thing.

*leans back, looking at ceiling*

My doctor's office didn't reach out before my appointment. I called to schedule it myself. I had to remember to make the appointment, had to take time off work we can't really afford to lose right now because I'm still job hunting. No one said, "As someone thinking about conception, here's what we recommend." I had to know to ask.

*voice becomes more animated, tinged with frustration*

When I went in, I tried to bring things up—my anxiety about fetal development, my concerns about being far from family. I had to work to steer the conversation there. And even then, my doctor seemed... content to just reassure me quickly and move on. She didn't dig deeper. She didn't ask follow-up questions.

*rubs face*

The system is very passive, I think. It assumes that if you're healthy, you'll figure it out. That you have resources, support, knowledge already in place. But I don't. I'm new here, I'm alone, I'm trying to navigate both immigration and starting a family at the same time.

*voice drops*

No one proactively offered me counseling, or a support group, or even a pamphlet about emotional readiness. No one said, "It sounds like you're isolated—let me connect you with other women." I had to discover everything myself, through Google and late-night reading.

*looks directly at you*

Strong advocacy? I didn't even know I was supposed to be advocating. I just thought... they would see me.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

*softens immediately, a tender expression crossing face*

I expected... partnership, I think. Real partnership.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

In my mind, we would go to appointments together. We would read the same information, ask questions side by side. I imagined sitting with my wife and talking about what we were learning—about fetal development, about preparing our bodies and our home. It felt like something we would do *together*, not something I would do alone while she worked.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

The reality is that my wife has her own anxiety right now. She's job hunting too, trying to establish herself here. When I bring up my fears about pregnancy, she listens—she's kind and supportive—but I can feel the weight on her. She's already stretched thin.

*looks down at hands*

I haven't asked her to come to my appointment. Part of me wanted to, but I knew she couldn't get the time off. Part of me also... I don't know. I didn't want to burden her with my fears. So I went alone, and I came home and tried to summarize what the doctor said, but it felt hollow. Like I was translating something that needed to be experienced together.

*voice cracks slightly*

What I expected was a shared journey. What's actually happening is that I'm carrying most of the emotional weight myself, while she carries the practical weight of finding work. We're both trying so hard to build something here that we don't have much left for each other some days.

*pauses*

I love her deeply. But I'm lonely even with her.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

*sits in silence for a moment, then nods slowly*

Every day. Every single day.

*voice becomes firm*

The burden is entirely on me. I'm the one who will carry the pregnancy. I'm the one whose body will change, whose health is being monitored. So yes, logically, more of the preparation falls to me. But it's more than that.

*stands up slightly, then sits back down, agitated*

There's this invisible pressure—as a woman, as an immigrant, as someone starting over. I feel like I have to be the keeper of this knowledge, the protector of this process. My wife, she trusts that things will work out. She's more... relaxed about it all. But for me, there's this weight that says I need to know everything, control everything, prepare perfectly so nothing goes wrong.

*voice becomes more introspective*

Part of it is cultural, I think. Back home, pregnancy is a woman's domain—you learn from your mother, your aunts, the women around you. That knowledge passes down through us. Here, I don't have that. So I've had to become my own authority. I read obstetric journals late at night. I trace diagrams of neural development like I'm memorizing a prayer.

*rubs eyes*

And my partner... she loves me, but she can't fill that gap. She can't be my mother or my sisters. She can't sit with me in the waiting room and hold my hand through the anxiety. She's dealing with her own displacement.

*voice quiets*

So the burden fell on me because there was no one else to carry it. And I think... I think I accepted that too easily.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

*pauses, shaking head gently*

I haven't experienced a loss. This is my first time trying. Sometimes I wonder if that makes my anxiety seem... silly? Like, I don't have a "real" reason to be this afraid.

*voice becomes hesitant*

But I think that's part of why I'm so anxious, actually. I don't have experience with this. I don't know what my body can do or what might go wrong. The unknown feels enormous.

*looks away*

I've read stories online—women who lost pregnancies and came back trying again. The fear in their words is so raw, so justified. And I think, at least they *know* something. They know their bodies can conceive. They know the pain of loss, yes, but also the reality of it.

*voice becomes quieter*

I'm afraid of things that haven't happened, and sometimes that feels less legitimate than being afraid of things that have. My doctor certainly treats it that way—she's never offered grief counseling or loss support groups, but I wonder if she would have if I'd been through that.

*rubs arms*

It's strange. Not having a loss means I'm "low risk," which should be good. But it also means I'm alone with these abstract terrors. I don't have a community of women who understand what I'm carrying—the weight of infinite possibility and infinite fear.

*pauses*

Sometimes I think having survived something would give me permission to ask for help. Right now, I'm just... anxious for no reason anyone can see.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

*looks confused, then slightly defensive*

I... I didn't have a previous loss. I said that already.

*pauses, uncertain*

Are you asking me what if? Or did I somehow... did I not explain clearly?

*shifts uncomfortably*

No, there's no previous loss. This is my first time trying to conceive. Maybe that's why I feel so lost—I have no reference point, no experience to ground me. Everything is theoretical, hypothetical, imagined.

*voice becomes smaller*

Sometimes I wonder if that's why no one is taking my anxiety seriously. If I had lost a baby, maybe my fear would seem more real to people. Maybe my doctor would listen differently. But because nothing has happened to me yet, because I'm just a healthy thirty-three-year-old with anxiety about things that haven't occurred...

*trails off*

I think that's part of my fear, actually. That I'm catastrophizing about something that might never happen. That my anxiety is unfounded and I should just be grateful I'm healthy and able to try.

*looks directly at you*

But the fear is real, even if the loss isn't. Does that make sense? The isolation is real. The longing for my mother is real. The pressure to succeed here, to build roots and a family—that's real too.

*pauses*

I'm sorry if I was unclear before.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a deep breath, eyes becoming slightly glassy*

Yes. There is something.

*pauses, gathering thoughts*

I want to say that I'm grateful for my health, truly. I know that's a privilege. But I also want whoever reads this to understand that being healthy isn't enough. Being "low risk" isn't enough. There's a person inside this body—inside me—who is terrified and alone and trying to plant roots in unfamiliar soil.

*voice becomes stronger, more insistent*

I need healthcare providers to see beyond the numbers on a chart. To understand that some of us don't have mothers nearby, don't have sisters, don't have a community waiting to catch us. We're building everything from scratch—a life in a new country, a marriage in isolation, now a family. The clinical reassurance doesn't touch that.

*leans forward*

And I want to say that the way I'm feeling right now, before I'm even pregnant—this anxiety, this grief, this longing—it matters. It should matter to my doctor. It should prompt real conversation, real support, not just a checkbox and a smile.

*voice softens*

I'm ready to be a mother. I think I am. But I'm also drowning, and no one seems to notice because the drowning is invisible. If something could change, I would ask for someone to see the whole person, not just the healthy body.

*pauses, then adds quietly*

And maybe... a support group for women like me. Immigrants trying to conceive far from home. That would mean everything.
